caveats: a: The occupation source combines farm, ranch, and aquaculture workers nationally and is not specific to shellfish farming.
a: BLS occupational employment data exclude self-employed workers, an important omission for small farms.
a: Sensor feasibility does not establish economic adoption or task-level time savings across farms.
rho: No industry-specific implementation cohort or return-on-investment study was found.
rho: Implementation will vary sharply by farm scale, species, site connectivity, and regulatory jurisdiction.
e: The Census of Aquaculture counts farms and sales, not LMM firms, normalized EBITDA, or control eligibility.
e: One firm may operate multiple sites or farms, so farm counts are not a firm denominator.
s5: The source covers all agricultural producers, not shellfish operators or LMM firms.
s5: Producer counts include multiple producers per farm and do not measure control transactions.
s5: Permits and water leases can make otherwise viable operations difficult to transfer.
q: No observed pass-through or post-improvement repricing study for shellfish farms was found.
q: Retention likely differs materially between branded direct sales, restoration contracts, distributors, and commodity wholesale channels.
d5: The USDA comparisons are nominal sales, not constant-price quantity.
d5: The 2023 Census of Aquaculture covers all aquaculture and reports species groups broader than the exact lens.
d5: Disease, harmful algal blooms, extreme weather, and ocean acidification can impair supply even when customer demand remains strong.
o: This is a bounded judgment rather than an observed operator-share measure.
o: The estimate concerns operator requirement, not employment levels; substantial labor automation can coexist with a high value.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is mainly per-pound, per-bushel, per-count, or lot pricing for live or processed-at-harvest shellfish, sometimes supplemented by seed, nursery, restoration, or contract-growing revenue. Relationships may repeat, but prices and volumes remain exposed to biological yield, seasonality, commodity competition, and buyer mix.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default service lens is narrowed to farms with repeat external supply or contract-growing relationships. Ordinary product sales remain economically central, so this is a lens mismatch rather than a conventional outsourced-service market; that limitation is retained explicitly rather than selecting only unusually attractive restoration or hatchery niches.
lens.excluded_activities: Wild harvest, finfish farming, seafood processing, restaurants, distributors, hobby farms, captive internal production, nonrecurring asset sales, and businesses whose economics are primarily land or permit speculation rather than recurring shellfish output.
lens.included_activities: US shellfish-farming businesses that repeatedly supply farmed oysters, clams, mussels, shrimp, crayfish, or similar shellfish to external wholesale, processor, distributor, restaurant, retail, restoration, or contract customers and otherwise satisfy the screen's ownership and size conditions.

a.bridge: BLS describes the national occupation Farmworkers, Farm, Ranch, and Aquacultural Animals, not the wage-weighted task mix of NAICS 112512, and it excludes self-employed workers. I translate the listed mix of feeding, animal-health observation, loading, recordkeeping, and equipment work into a modest exposure share, supported directionally by NOAA's demonstration that shellfish sensors can automate continuous biological monitoring. This likely overstates exposure where owner labor and hands-on field work dominate, but may understate it at larger hatcheries with substantial monitoring, planning, sales, and compliance work.
caveats: The occupation source combines farm, ranch, and aquaculture workers nationally and is not specific to shellfish farming.
BLS occupational employment data exclude self-employed workers, an important omission for small farms.
Sensor feasibility does not establish economic adoption or task-level time savings across farms.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from a representative sample of oyster, clam, mussel, and crustacean farms, split between owners and employees and recording current software and sensor use, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Most current work is physical husbandry, handling, maintenance, and harvesting, while the more substitutable opportunity sits in monitoring, recordkeeping, scheduling, inventory, customer communication, and environmental decision support. NOAA's real-time biosensor work demonstrates a concrete monitoring pathway but not an industry adoption rate [S6].

d5.bridge: USDA measures nominal aquaculture sales between the 2018 and 2023 censuses, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity for the narrowed recurring-customer shellfish service basket. I use the reported 30% increase in mollusk sales and 75% increase in crustacean sales as evidence of market expansion, then reduce the base materially for inflation, product mix, species composition, and the fact that sales can rise without physical quantity. This likely overstates real quantity growth when price increases dominate, but may understate durable demand where domestic supply replaces imports or premium species expand.
caveats: The USDA comparisons are nominal sales, not constant-price quantity.
The 2023 Census of Aquaculture covers all aquaculture and reports species groups broader than the exact lens.
Disease, harmful algal blooms, extreme weather, and ocean acidification can impair supply even when customer demand remains strong.
d5.change_evidence: Species-level five-year production pounds or counts matched to deflated farm-gate prices, plus committed restoration and wholesale volumes, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: US aquaculture sales reached $1.9 billion in 2023, up 26% from 2018; mollusk sales were $575.5 million, up 30%, and crustacean sales $175.7 million, up 75% [S2]. NOAA also describes marine shellfish as a cornerstone of domestic marine aquaculture [S3]. These indicators support positive demand, but constant-price quantity is not observed.

caveats: The Census of Aquaculture counts farms and sales, not LMM firms, normalized EBITDA, or control eligibility.
One firm may operate multiple sites or farms, so farm counts are not a firm denominator.
e.change_evidence: A business-level census or licensed registry linking farm operators to revenue, normalized EBITDA, ownership, customer contracts, and operating status would permit an eligible-share estimate.
e.rationale: The frozen dataset provides no defensible LMM firm-count denominator, and no roster was found that jointly identifies shellfish-farming business model, normalized EBITDA, independence, and recurring external-customer eligibility. Estimating a share without that denominator would create false precision.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a bounded judgment rather than an observed operator-share measure.
The estimate concerns operator requirement, not employment levels; substantial labor automation can coexist with a high value.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of fully autonomous commercial farms, material customer insourcing of cultivation, synthetic substitutes displacing farmed shellfish, or permit structures allowing software-only supply would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five shellfish quantity still requires a licensed, accountable farm operator to manage biological production, leases or permits, food safety, harvesting, and customer fulfillment. Software, sensors, and autonomous equipment can reduce visits and labor but do not eliminate cultivation or transfer the whole current service basket to customer self-service.

caveats: No observed pass-through or post-improvement repricing study for shellfish farms was found.
Retention likely differs materially between branded direct sales, restoration contracts, distributors, and commodity wholesale channels.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level contract samples showing price-reset clauses, channel mix, gross-margin persistence, buyer concentration, and realized retention of operating improvements after renewals would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: A farm initially retains savings from better scheduling, monitoring, mortality prevention, and administrative productivity, but buyers can recapture part through negotiated prices, competitive bidding, or renewal. Differentiation by species, quality, provenance, freshness, and dependable supply supports partial retention, while commodity-like channels and distributor concentration reduce it.

caveats: No industry-specific implementation cohort or return-on-investment study was found.
Implementation will vary sharply by farm scale, species, site connectivity, and regulatory jurisdiction.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year adoption and realized-hours-saved data from shellfish-farm management vendors, sensor providers, insurers, extension programs, and a panel of farm operators would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Within five years, farms can plausibly implement office automation, environmental alerts, production forecasting, record generation, and customer workflows, but physical work remains difficult to automate. Exposure to water conditions, device fouling, sparse connectivity, food-safety controls, permitting, limited technical capacity, and small-farm economics constrain implementation. NOAA documents real-time monitoring as useful for adaptation but does not measure commercial implementation rates [S6][S8].

s5.bridge: USDA's Census of Agriculture age statistics cover all US producers rather than owners of eligible LMM shellfish farms. I use the high prevalence of older producers as directional evidence of succession pressure, then discount heavily because age does not imply a qualifying control sale, many shellfish farms may have different demographics, and family transfer or closure may replace an external transaction. The proxy likely overstates qualifying transfers if small farms are nontransferable, but could understate them where permits, leases, and established buyer relationships attract consolidators.
caveats: The source covers all agricultural producers, not shellfish operators or LMM firms.
Producer counts include multiple producers per farm and do not measure control transactions.
Permits and water leases can make otherwise viable operations difficult to transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Shellfish-specific owner-age and succession surveys, permit-transfer records by state, and a five-year transaction census from brokers and aquaculture lenders would materially change this range.
s5.rationale: USDA reports that 38% of US producers were age 65 or older in 2022 and the average age was 58.1, creating plausible five-year succession pressure [S5]. The broad range reflects the missing shellfish-specific owner-age distribution and the conversion from succession events to qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Durable, operator-required biological production with tangible AI-enabled monitoring and planning use cases.
principal_weakness: No defensible count or eligibility share of transferable LMM firms, compounded by a product-selling rather than outsourced-service business model.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as farm raising of shellfish such as crayfish, shrimp, oysters, clams, and mollusks and distinguishes it from related activities.
sources.title: NAICS Sector 11 Definitions: 112512 Shellfish Farming

sources.supports: Reports 2023 US aquaculture sales of $1.9 billion, 26% above 2018, 3,453 farms, and category sales and growth for mollusks and crustaceans.
sources.title: USDA Releases the 2023 Census of Aquaculture Results

sources.supports: Reports 2023 US aquaculture production and value and describes marine shellfish as a cornerstone of US marine aquaculture.
sources.title: Fisheries of the United States

sources.supports: Describes shellfish production, permit requirements, and 2023 oyster, clam, and mussel output and estimated value.
sources.title: Understanding Shellfish Aquaculture

sources.supports: Reports an average US producer age of 58.1 and that 38% of producers were age 65 or older.
sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture Highlights: Farm Producers

sources.supports: Documents open-source biosensors measuring shellfish gaping and heart rate in real time and describes how automated monitoring can reduce reliance on in-person observations.
sources.title: Shellfish Biosensors for Real-Time Monitoring

sources.supports: Lists core duties including feeding, health observation, loading, equipment upkeep, and records, providing a broad occupation-task proxy.
sources.title: Occupational Employment and Wages: Farmworkers, Farm, Ranch, and Aquacultural Animals

sources.supports: Describes real-time ocean data used by shellfish growers to adapt operations and documents the operational threat posed by corrosive seawater.
sources.title: Pacific Ocean Acidification Observing Project

title: Shellfish Farming
